edges.description: CHD5 dysfunction alters chromatin regulation during brain development, leading to abnormal cortical maturation and neuronal circuit formation.
Abnormal neuronal development and circuit organization increase seizure susceptibility.
Abnormal cortical development manifests as global developmental delay.
Persistent neurodevelopmental impairment causes lifelong intellectual disability.
Disrupted cortical development contributes to prominent language deficits.
Abnormal neuronal network development lowers the threshold for epilepsy.
Cortical and synaptic dysfunction contributes to autistic, obsessive, and aggressive behavioral features.

nodes.description: Antiseizure treatment and neurology follow-up should be individualized to seizure type, because epilepsy is common and includes West syndrome, generalized tonic-clonic seizures, and other seizure types.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: Epilepsy types were variable, with West syndrome observed in three patients, generalized tonic-clonic seizures in two, and other subtypes observed in one individual each.

nodes.description: Behavioral disturbances include autism-spectrum features, obsessive- compulsive tendencies, aggression, and social cognition deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: The most common clinical features included language deficits (81%), behavioral symptoms (69%), intellectual disability (64%), epilepsy (62%), and motor delay (56%).

evidence.reference_title: New Insights into the Relation between Cognition, Behavior, and the CHD5 Gene: A Case-Report of an Adult Male with Parenti-Mignot Neurodevelopmental Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Parenti-Mignot neurodevelopmental syndrome is caused by pathogenic variants of the CHD5 gene - involved in brain development - and is characterized by developmental delay, intellectual disability, and behavioral disturbances (i.e., autism spectrum disorder or related social problems, obsessive-compulsive tendencies, and aggressive behavior) as well as subtle facial dysmorphisms, epilepsy, hypotonia, and craniosynostosis.

nodes.description: Behavioral and psychiatric supports should be guided by formal neuropsychological profiling, especially for social cognition weaknesses, obsessive-compulsive tendencies, aggression, mood problems, and emotion regulation deficits.

evidence.reference_title: New Insights into the Relation between Cognition, Behavior, and the CHD5 Gene: A Case-Report of an Adult Male with Parenti-Mignot Neurodevelopmental Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: In clinical practice, neuropsychological assessment can provide helpful pointers for treatment and support in daily functioning.

evidence.reference_title: New Insights into the Relation between Cognition, Behavior, and the CHD5 Gene: A Case-Report of an Adult Male with Parenti-Mignot Neurodevelopmental Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Additionally, results indicated internalizing and externalizing behavioral problems and deficits in emotion regulation skills.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: Thanks to GeneMatcher and international collaborations, we assembled a cohort of 16 unrelated individuals harboring heterozygous CHD5 variants, all identified by exome sequencing.

nodes.description: Pathogenic CHD5 variants disrupt chromodomain helicase DNA-binding protein 5 function within the nucleosome remodeling and deacetylation (NuRD) complex, impairing chromatin remodeling programs required for neuronal development.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: Located in the critical 1p36 microdeletion region, the chromodomain helicase DNA-binding protein 5 (CHD5) gene encodes a subunit of the nucleosome remodeling and deacetylation (NuRD) complex required for neuronal development.

nodes.description: Individuals with suspected craniosynostosis should be referred for craniofacial evaluation; surgical management is phenotype-directed when premature suture fusion causes functional or cranial growth concerns.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: Three familial cases had nonsense or missense variants segregating with speech delay, learning disabilities, and/or craniosynostosis.

nodes.description: Craniosynostosis occurs in some affected individuals and may prompt early craniofacial evaluation.

evidence.reference_title: New Insights into the Relation between Cognition, Behavior, and the CHD5 Gene: A Case-Report of an Adult Male with Parenti-Mignot Neurodevelopmental Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Parenti-Mignot neurodevelopmental syndrome is caused by pathogenic variants of the CHD5 gene - involved in brain development - and is characterized by developmental delay, intellectual disability, and behavioral disturbances (i.e., autism spectrum disorder or related social problems, obsessive-compulsive tendencies, and aggressive behavior) as well as subtle facial dysmorphisms, epilepsy, hypotonia, and craniosynostosis.

nodes.description: CHD5 dysfunction impairs cortical brain development and neuronal circuit maturation, producing cognitive and language deficits.

evidence.reference_title: New Insights into the Relation between Cognition, Behavior, and the CHD5 Gene: A Case-Report of an Adult Male with Parenti-Mignot Neurodevelopmental Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: CHD5 involvement in cortical brain development may be an explanation for these cognitive deficits.

nodes.description: Epilepsy is a common feature with variable seizure semiology.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: Epilepsy types were variable, with West syndrome observed in three patients, generalized tonic-clonic seizures in two, and other subtypes observed in one individual each.

nodes.description: CHD5-related neurodevelopmental abnormalities also affect seizure threshold and epileptogenic network organization.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: The most common clinical features included language deficits (81%), behavioral symptoms (69%), intellectual disability (64%), epilepsy (62%), and motor delay (56%).

evidence.reference_title: New Insights into the Relation between Cognition, Behavior, and the CHD5 Gene: A Case-Report of an Adult Male with Parenti-Mignot Neurodevelopmental Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Parenti-Mignot neurodevelopmental syndrome is caused by pathogenic variants of the CHD5 gene - involved in brain development - and is characterized by developmental delay, intellectual disability, and behavioral disturbances (i.e., autism spectrum disorder or related social problems, obsessive-compulsive tendencies, and aggressive behavior) as well as subtle facial dysmorphisms, epilepsy, hypotonia, and craniosynostosis.

nodes.description: Intellectual disability is a defining feature of the syndrome and often persists into adulthood.

evidence.reference_title: Missense and truncating variants in CHD5 in a dominant neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual disability, behavioral disturbances, and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: The most common clinical features included language deficits (81%), behavioral symptoms (69%), intellectual disability (64%), epilepsy (62%), and motor delay (56%).

nodes.description: Language deficits are especially prominent and are often one of the most noticeable manifestations.

evidence.snippet: The most common clinical features included language deficits (81%), behavioral symptoms (69%), intellectual disability (64%), epilepsy (62%), and motor delay (56%).
